What I did today: Today, I worked on the bug ticket rocks-3790: Client Portal - Saved data is not persisted when the user logs out and logs back in. I spent the day investigating the reported issue and later confirmed that the current behavior is functioning as designed. The workflow intentionally stores form data in local storage to prevent data loss when the modal dialog is accidentally closed, rather than persisting the data across user sessions. Based on the video provided by the QA, he/she didn't proceed with submitting the form, then she/he logged out. Obviously, the data has been deleted from the local storage. When she/he visited the page and opened the form, her/his previous data was lost. The ticket is now in code review. What I did today: Working with [LOC-6814] fix: HttpGateway prefer IPv6 with automatic fallback to IPv4 on network timeoutshttps://github.com/getflywheel/flywheel-local/pull/2346 Local's Electron/Node networking was intermittently failing to reach WordPress.org endpoints (translations + version check) with ETIMEDOUT during TLS connect, even when the same URLs were reachable via curl. This PR updates the shared HTTP layer to be more resilient on networks with broken/partial IPv6 (or otherwise flaky routing) by preferring IPv6 when it works and automatically falling back to IPv4 when IPv6 requests fail with connectivity-style errors.

What I did today: Ticket number: 1212639771440661 Task summary: Work on Change History Tab Progress: WIP Outcome: - Removed LaunchDarkly feature flag guards from the three change-history API endpoints (GetChangeHistory, GetChangeHistoryDetail, RevertDemographicChange), which were causing 404s. - Added RevertedToDate column to SurveyDemographicChangeLog (migration script, entity property, EF config, command record, query DTO, and history query). - Rewrote the revert handler to roll back ALL entries newer than the target - supporting both day-aggregated one-off edits and single bulk imports - and record a single RevertChange entry stamped with RevertedToDate. - Filtered readonly demographic types (Generation, Tenure Range, Immediate Manager) from change recording by extracting IsUserEditable() into DemographicTypeExtensions as a shared extension method. - Rebuilt ChangeHistory.tsx: 4-column table (Date / Edit+details / User / Current Version or Revert), day-aggregate grouping with correct most-recent-first sort, "Reverted to {date}" label for revert entries, timestamp tooltip on Date column, Voyager confirmation modal. - Wired cross-tab refresh: after a successful revert, ChangeHistory clears participantsAtom directly; DemographicManagement detects the atom going empty-while-not-loading via a ref and refetches - avoiding a double request when the user had paginated past page 1. - Refactored ChangeHistory.tsx into sub-components (UserAvatar, ChangeHistoryRow, RevertModal) with pure helpers in a separate section, reducing the main component to state + orchestration only.
What I will be doing the next working da: Work on bug in bulk demographic import upload

What I did today: Ticket number: 1212639771440652 Task summary: Resolve PR comments for bulk import demographics Progress: WIP Outcome: Bulk Demographic Import - Phase 1/2 Refactor Restructured the participant demographic bulk import into two distinct phases to ensure no database writes occur before the user confirms: - Phase 1 (calculate) - now fully read-only. Reads the uploaded Excel file, compares each cell against existing SurveyDemographicValue.TextValue1 values, and produces a planned diff stored in the job's JSON payload. New text values (not yet in the DB) are carried as TextValue+DemographicType on the change record for deferred creation. - Phase 2 (confirm) - all DB writes happen here on user confirmation. Creates any new CompanyDemographicValueMapping and SurveyDemographicValue rows needed, resolves them to codes, then applies all participant slot updates in batches. Bug in progress - re-uploading an unchanged file incorrectly reports all Job Title values as planned changes. Root cause identified: the lookup was comparing against CompanyDemographicValueMapping.Text (a cleaned/normalized field) instead of SurveyDemographicValue.TextValue1 (the exact text the export file produces). A fix was attempted by switching to a direct TextValue1 comparison, but the issue persists and is still under investigation. Rename - ImportParticipantDemographicsHandler.cs renamed to CalculateParticipantDemographicImportChangesHandler.cs (and both classes inside it) to distinguish Phase 1 from Phase 2 by name.

What I did today: Ticket number: 1212639771440661 Task summary: Work on Change History Tab Progress: WIP Outcome: Work on batching updates for change history and using RevertedToId instead of RevertedToDate in react page. Demographics Change History - RevertedToDate → RevertedToId Replaced the RevertedToDate (DateTimeOffset?) field on SurveyDemographicChangeLog with RevertedToId (long?), which now stores the SurveyDemographicChangeLog.Id of the version being reverted to rather than its timestamp. Backend changes: - DB migration adds RevertedToId BIGINT NULL and drops RevertedToDate (release9.09/0003) - Domain model, EF config, command, query model, and Dapper query all updated to use the new field - Revert handler passes revertedToId: request.ChangeLogId - no extra query needed because the frontend now sends the earliest Id in an aggregate directly Frontend changes (ChangeHistory.tsx): - primaryEntry in DisplayGroup is now always the earliest (lowest Id) entry in an aggregate group - the if (entry.id > existing.primaryEntry.id) existing.primaryEntry = entry update was removed - Group sorting and isCurrentVersion now use the last entry in entries[] (the highest Id) since primaryEntry no longer serves that role - revert(primaryEntry.id) naturally sends the earliest Id in a DemographicEdited aggregate, or the single entry's Id for non-aggregatable types - formatChangeType now resolves the display date by looking up the referenced entry by revertedToId via a historyById map, replacing the old direct revertedToDate string read Also completed earlier in this session: - BulkUpdateParticipantDemographicsRequestHandler - new handler that batches all survey-level DB fetches once, resolves all demographic codes in a two-pass pre-loop (at most two SaveChanges calls total), and writes all participant updates in a single repository call; added RecordChangeHistory = true parameter to allow callers (e.g., future revert integration) to suppress the internal BulkEdit history entry
What I will be doing the next working da: Work bulk import handling in change history in demographics react page.

What I did today: Ticket number: 1212639771440661 Task summary: Work on Change History Tab Progress: Done Outcome: 1. Bulk imports now appear in Change History - Demographic imports made via the CSV upload flow are recorded as changelog entries alongside manual edits, visible in the Change History UI.2. Imports are now revertible - Because they share the same changelog format, any import can be undone via the existing RevertSurveyDemographicChangeCommand with no additional work.3. BulkUpdateParticipantDemographicsRequestHandler is now the single path for all bulk demographic writes - Manual bulk edits, imports, and reverts all go through one handler, eliminating duplicate logic for PIT locking, org tree updates, change history recording, and GoalPost sync.4. No frontend changes required - The existing ChangeHistory.tsx UI surfaces import entries automatically.5. Handler no longer depends on HttpContext or session - CallerContext is required at the call site, making the handler safe to call from any Hangfire background job without lifecycle concerns.

UB-9404: Yesterday, I encountered a checkout error in the CS environment (v25.52) that prevented me from proceeding to promotions. Today, debugging revealed that it's related to the RegexValidationOptions stored procedure, where X2_SHIPPING_SHIP_PHONE_VALIDATE_REGEX is set to 0 for both US and Default territories. Since the stored procedure returns no rows, this causes a Dapper multi-mapping error due to a missing expected ID (splitOn issue). This may also affect other clients, according to Clarisse. As a temporary workaround, I updated the stored procedure to return an empty result set with schema only, allowing checkout to proceed to the promotions/discounts step since the setting is disabled. What I did today: Attended daily stand up. nVision service request 254949. FIndings: setQueryData requires an exact query key match. The read side caches under a 6-element key (including filter), but the write side constructed a 5-element key. No match found, updater old gets undefined. Fix: Switched to setQueriesData, which does partial key matching. The mutation now matches all cached variants of project list (regardless of sort/filter) and successfully updates the favorite state in one operation. Submitted PR. Dan has a comment about the approach.

What I did today: [SEN-2152] What is the impact to Coyote when Let's Encrypt certificate lifetime changes? Work completed Reviewed the Let's Encrypt announcement "Decreasing Certificate Lifetimes to 45 Days" to understand upcoming changes to certificate lifetimes and authorization reuse. Audited the Coyote codebase and configuration to determine: which ACME protocol version is used, whether ACME profiles are explicitly configured, how certificate issuance and renewal are currently handled. Reviewed the acme-client gem source to verify whether ACME profile selection is supported and whether Coyote uses that capability. Key findings / outcomes Let's Encrypt will reduce certificate lifetimes in phases: tlsserver profile → 45 days (opt-in, May 13, 2026) Default classic profile → 64 days (Feb 10, 2027) and 45 days (Feb 16, 2028) Authorization reuse windows will also be significantly reduced. Coyote: Uses ACME v2 and the Let's Encrypt v2 directory. Does not explicitly configure or persist an ACME profile. Relies on default (classic) Let's Encrypt behavior. Although the ACME client gem supports passing a profile during order creation, Coyote does not currently pass or configure a profile. Result: No impact from the May 13, 2026 opt-in (tlsserver) change Automatic impact expected when default classic profile changes in 2027 and 2028 Flywheel and SimpleSSL are impacted indirectly, as they fully depend on Coyote for SSL issuance and renewal.
What I will be doing the next working da: [SEN-2152] What is the impact to Coyote when Let's Encrypt certificate lifetime changes? Address AC: Document and plan any actions that need to be taken (if any)

What I did today: [SEN-2152] What is the impact to Coyote when Let's Encrypt certificate lifetime changes? Document and plan any actions that need to be taken Based on the Let's Encrypt certificate lifetime changes, no immediate code changes are required. Coyote's current renewal logic remains functionally correct: renew when 30 days remain (expiring_soon) checked hourly As an interim mitigation aligned with Let's Encrypt's recommended two-thirds renewal timing, we plan to adjust the expiring_soon renewal threshold as certificate lifetimes shorten: February 10, 2027 (64-day certificates): update expiring_soon to 22 days February 16, 2028 (45-day certificates): update expiring_soon to 15 days These values ensure certificates are renewed at approximately two-thirds of their lifetime, maintaining a safe renewal buffer while avoiding unnecessary early re-orders. This approach minimizes code changes and keeps renewal behavior aligned with Let's Encrypt guidance. What I did today: [SEN-2085] [Tracking] - getflywheel/flywheel-app (via dependabot) - Bump rack to 3.1.17 Investigated error NoMethodError: undefined method `include?' for nil This error was caused by a one-off scanner request attempting host-header injection using a malformed forwarded host value. Rack rejected the invalid host, resulting in request.hostbeingnil, and a rack-attackthrottle rule assumed the host was always present and called.include?`. The request did not target a valid route, occurred only once since deployment, and did not affect any real users or system behavior. As this was isolated bot activity with no impact, it does not require remediation at this time. Investigated error Rack::Multipart::EmptyContentError: Rack::Multipart::EmptyContentError This error is triggered by automated bots sending malformed multipart/form-data POST requests to random, non-existent URLs. After the recent upgrade to Rack 3, Rack now strictly validates multipart requests and raises an exception when a multipart body contains empty or invalid parts, whereas older versions silently ignored this. The requests never reach valid Rails routes or application logic and originate from background scanner traffic, not real users. Honeybadger shows no affected users and no functional impact, so this is considered harmless monitoring noise and does not require action. What I did today: [SEN-2085] [Tracking] - getflywheel/flywheel-app (via dependabot) - Bump rack to 3.1.17 Investigated an issue where O2C cannot issue refunds on FW, findings: Previously, Rack 2 was able to parse the query string `a[b[c]]=x` in the same way as `a[b][c]=x`. This invalid syntax was never officially supported. However, some libraries and applications used it anyway. Due to implementation details, Rack 2 ended up parsing it the same as the correct syntax. The implementation was changed in Rack 3, and this invalid syntax is no longer parsed the same way as the correct syntax: Rack::Utils.parse_nested_query("a[b[c]]=x")# Rack 3 => {"a"=>{"b[c"=>{"]"=>"x"}}} ❌# Rack 2 => {"a"=>{"b"=>{"c"=>"x"}}} ✅ The correct syntax for nested parameters is a[b][c]=x and we will need to change that in FW code to be compatible with Rack 3: Rack::Utils.parse_nested_query("a[b][c]=x")# Rack 3 => {"a"=>{"b"=>{"c"=>"x"}}} ✅# Rack 2 => {"a"=>{"b"=>{"c"=>"x"}}} ✅
What I will be doing the next working da: [SEN-2085] [Tracking] - getflywheel/flywheel-app (via dependabot) - Bump rack to 3.1.17 Look for the invalid syntax `a[b[c]]=x` in FW code and change it to valid syntax `a[b][c]=x`. Also create an alternative solution whether to port Rack 2 parsing method or to monkey-patch Rack 3 to accept invalid syntax.

[SEN-2276] Investigated the inability to cancel subscriptions A customer (org 11962) was unable to cancel Growth Suite client subscriptions, receiving an "Uh oh, there was a problem cancelling your subscription" error. The root cause was a data inconsistency-seven client subscriptions were still marked as "active" in Growth Suite but no longer existed in Stripe, which caused the cancellation flow to fail when it attempted to reach Stripe. This was resolved by updating the statuses of the seven affected subscriptions from "active" to "canceled" directly in the database.

What I did today: [SEN-2288] Fix GrowthSuite::Datasource::Sites::FetchDatasourceSiteError The GrowthSuite::Datasource::Sites::FetchDatasourceSiteError spike seen in Honeybadger after the Ruby 3.3.8 → 3.4.2 production deploy is not a regression introduced by the upgrade The error was already occurring before the deploy - confirmed by a matching pre-deploy fault (HB #93463640, Feb 17) with identical timing and failure path The apparent spike is a Honeybadger regrouping artifact: the Ruby upgrade rewrote the GlobalIDServerMiddleware (yield moved from line 17 → 28) and Ruby 3.4 changed backtrace formatting, causing all post-deploy occurrences to accumulate under a new fault ID (#127591700) and inflate the count No code fix is required on our end - the error handling is working as designed and the source of the failures is outside our control
What I will be doing the next working da: I will continue investigate HB errors introduced after the ruby upgrade and will also help on Hansel's ticket if needed.

What I did today: [SEN-2301] Follow up: Admin missing Hubspot fields Issue: 67% of users are missing Hubspot owner/account manager data in the Flywheel admin panel. Root Cause: The Hubspot API was broken from February to March 2026. During the outage, owner assignments in Hubspot were not synced to Flywheel. The daily PULL job only syncs contacts modified in the last 25 hours, resulting in missed changes becoming permanently stale. Solution Implemented: A manual sync script was created to query all Hubspot contacts via the API and update Flywheel users with the current owner/account manager and assigned date.

What I did today: Findings for SEN-2358 - Investigate March Dyno Usage Increase for Flywheel The March dyno overage was caused by an intentional bug fix that unblocked a multi-year backlog of vulnerability notification emails. A NULL NOT IN SQL bug in Vulnerabilities::NotificationOwnersQuery had been silently excluding all User-owned site owners from vulnerability notifications since approximately May 2022 - any time even one Organization had vulnerability_email_optout enabled, Postgres' three-valued logic caused the query to drop the entire User-owned cohort. The fix landed in PR #17719 (commit 2cfbb315a0) and shipped in release v6227 on Mar 17 at 15:25 UTC, with a clean traceable chain from Zendesk #15455553 (customer reported missing emails since May 2022) through SREPROD-3325 to the merged PR. The hypothesis was confirmed against production via heroku pg:psql against the exploit_notifications table. Daily notification writes ran at a baseline of ~150/day from Feb 16 through Mar 16, then jumped to 27,567 on Mar 17 (the deploy day) and 44,795 on Mar 18 (the first full nightly run after the fix) - a near-perfect correlation with the deploy timestamp. Monthly totals tell the same story: roughly 2,300 notifications in late February, ~76,500 in March, and ~119,000 in April month-to-date. The failed_at column stayed at zero across the entire window, so this is real first-time send work rather than a retry storm. The mechanism translating notification volume into dyno-hours is the worker formation's autoscaler. The worker formation (Standard-2X) was observed scaling from 12 to 42 dynos in real time during this investigation. Pre-fix, the daily NotifyPluginExploitsWorker run was brief; post-fix, the same worker performs roughly two orders of magnitude more NotificationSitesQuery lookups and synchronous Notifier::Vulnerabilities.plugin(...).deliver_now calls inside a single find_each loop, while every-3h Patchstack syncs continuously feed new exploits into the funnel. Longer worker occupancy means a higher autoscaled dyno count, which is the +14.81 average dynos observed for March. No formation or scaling changes shipped via heroku ps:scale in Feb-Apr (heroku releases confirms zero "Scaled/Resized" entries), so the increase is entirely workload-driven, not capacity-driven. The Mar 17/18 mega-spike will not repeat - it was a one-time backlog burn-down of every (exploit, user-owned site, user) combination that had been suppressed since 2022, and the unique key on ExploitNotification ensures every combo is notified exactly once, ever. The new steady-state runs in punctuated equilibrium: roughly 25 quiet days per month (~100-400 notifications) plus 3-4 high-impact spike days (10k-45k notifications) when major plugin CVEs land via Patchstack and fan out across thousands of sites. That math lines up at ~100-120k notifications/month, matching the April MTD pace. Long-term, the steady-state will drift slowly upward - single-digit percent monthly - from organic customer growth, Patchstack's rising publication rate, and plugin version drift on existing sites; not exponentially. In summary, the March overage reflects correct, intentional behavior that was previously broken - not a regression, not unexpected growth, and not a transient spike. Recommended next steps are to (a) re-baseline the worker dyno budget against the April pace since the February baseline reflected the broken query rather than intended workload, and (b) optimize the notifier worker. The highest-leverage code change is to swap the daily schedule from NotifyPluginExploitsWorker to the existing NotifyExploitsWorker (already shaped to wrap per-exploit jobs in a Sidekiq::Batch), which would parallelize the work across the sidekiq formation instead of one long-running dyno; switching the mailer call to deliver_later is a smaller follow-up that further reduces worker occupancy time. What I did today: Vue 3 Client Portal Migration - Testing Report Setup Two git worktrees of the same repo: vue3-2 (the migration branch) and main (the baseline). Both stacks ran simultaneously on different ports (:3000 and :3001) so we could compare without checkout-juggling. A Playwright comparison harness at ~/Code/flywheel-compare/ ran the same scenarios against both stacks in one invocation. Snapshots tagged __vue3.png / __main.png so a diff script could pair and pixel-compare them. Workflow Recon - visited the page on both branches, diffed the relevant Vue component / HAML to spot the migration shape (vee-validate stub, mount-target trap, or component refactor). Spec - wrote a Playwright scenario file with a POST/PUT/DELETE trap that aborts mutating requests, so destructive submit handlers couldn't change the seeded test user / payment methods / subscriptions during testing. Run - both branches in one pass; Playwright captured screenshots + console + network events per branch. Diff - bin/diff.ts rebuilt reports/index.html with pixel-diff %, log-delta count, and a per-page status badge. Audit - each page got a markdown writeup (audits/<page>.md) with verdict, findings table (main vs vue3-2), root cause, and recommended fix shape from the migration playbook. Fix when broken - for FAIL rows, applied the playbook fix on vue3-2 locally (uncommitted), re-ran the spec, and confirmed parity (typically 0% pixel diff after). Mark - flipped task.md column 7 to P / F based on as-shipped behavior (the audit md captures the proposed fix separately). Test data discipline A single seeded test user (vue3-portal-tester@local.test) was used across all pages. When a page mutated state (password update, subscription replace, payment-method delete), the POST trap blocked the network call so the seeded record stayed intact across runs. Records that needed to exist (payment methods, subscription, invoice proposal, receipts) were seeded via Rails runner; cleanup snippets are in each audit md. Outputs ~/Code/flywheel-compare/reports/index.html - team-presentable summary table linking each audited page to its full markdown writeup and per-scenario screenshot triplet (vue3 / main / pixel-diff overlay). 12 audited pages, mapped 1:1 against the CSV row list. 4 regressions confirmed (/profile, /payment-methods/:id/edit, /subscriptions/:id/edit, /invoice_proposals/:id/accept), all 4 fixes applied to vue3-2 and verified, shipped as a single PR back to inaki/vue3-2.

What I did today: Released a new version of the converter has been released and will be applied on the next automatic update (5AM). Below is the release notes Release Notes - SOTA Database Converter v3 - Updated historical retrieval to discovers folders bottom-up (deepest first) and reads files - Converted from single-image sequential processing to a worker batch processing model, where each worker fetches batches of up to 1,000 images and processes them sequentially -- Thread-safe batch fetching via SemaphoreSlim prevents workers from claiming the same images -- Images are marked as Processing when claimed to prevent double-processing across workers -- Worker count scales dynamically based on configured off-peak hours (default: 9 PM - 6 AM) -- 3 workers during peak hours, 5 workers during off-peak hours (default and configurable) - Crash Recovery on startup, all images previously tagged as Processing are requeued back to Queued status, recovering from service restarts or crashes
What I will be doing the next working da: Monitor and review the updated machines and make sure no issue is encountered

What I did today: - **Replaced Windows Forms updater with headless BackgroundService**: Removed `UpdateForm` (WinForms-based) and replaced with `UpdateWorker`, a `BackgroundService` that runs unattended as a long-lived Windows service - no UI or user session required - **Scheduled daily update check at 5:00 AM**: Instead of running once and exiting (relying on Task Scheduler intervals), the service now stays running and calculates the delay until the next 5 AM, then checks again the following day - **Added full download and install logic**: `UpdateWorker` handles the complete update lifecycle - download with checksum validation, file type detection (ZIP/MSI/EXE), and installer execution with admin elevation support - **Truncate JSON log to 1000 characters**: Prevents oversized log payloads from failing the logging API - **Centralized assembly versioning**: Added `Directory.Build.props` at solution root with separate Debug (`1.0.0.1`) and Release (`1.0.0.40`) version configs, removed per-project `AssemblyVersion` entries - **Added `Updater` log type** (`LogType.Updater = 10`) for dedicated updater service logging - **Added `APPLICATION_SOFTWARE_NAME` constant** in `ConverterGlobalValues` to replace hardcoded `"SOTADatabaseV3Converter"` strings - **Added debug installer**: `SOTADatabaseV3Converter-debug.aip` for testing update flow - **Updated publish profiles** to target `x64` platform
What I will be doing the next working da: - [ ] Verify service starts and logs the next scheduled 5 AM check time - [ ] Confirm update check runs at 5:00 AM and logs results - [ ] After a check completes, verify it schedules the next check for the following day at 5 AM - [ ] Test with an available update: verify download, checksum validation, and MSI install launch - [ ] Test with no update available: verify "No update available" is logged - [ ] Stop the Windows service during sleep - verify it exits cleanly without errors - [ ] Verify log messages are truncated to 1000 characters - [ ] Confirm Debug build uses version `1.0.0.1` and Release uses `1.0.0.40` - [ ] Verify debug installer (`SOTADatabaseV3Converter-debug.aip`) builds and installs correctly

What I did today: - Fix updater issue- Corrected UpdateAt DateTimeOffset nullable update command- Set NormalWorkerCount default value to 2 and allow value to zero to prevent conversion during normal hours- Updated upload method to use async/await to prevent thread pool blocking and prevent thread starvation.- Updated StatusLog check then performed CreateLog HTTP Post outside to prevent worker blockint each other- Updated ThrottledStream to override ReadAsync to delegate the Memory<byte> overload properly and replaced Read() to its own synchronous throttle logic to eliminate sync-over-async deadlock risk.- Updated Cloud interface CreateLogs() and UpdateConversionStatusLog() methods to async- Replace the ConversionStatusLogList with a ConcurrentDictionary<Guid, ConversionStatusLog> keyed by ImageId. What I did today: ConvertImage Performance Overhaul - Changes Summary Files Modified - CloudImaging/Business Logic/Conversions/ConversionService.cs - CloudImaging/Business Logic/Conversions/ConversionImageDataHelper.cs - CloudImaging/Models/ConversionModels/ConversionProgressTracker.cs - CloudImaging/Startup.cs High-Impact Performance Fixes 1. Eliminated blob re-download for MD5 hashing CreateNewImage was calling StorageHelper.CalculateMD5HashAsync(blobClient) after every upload - streaming the entire image back from Azure just to hash it. MD5 is now computed client-side from the upload stream via CryptoStream, piggy-backing on the same bytes flowing to Azure. Zero extra network I/O. 2. Replaced System.Drawing.Image.FromStream with SkiaSharp CheckIfValidPixelData used System.Drawing.Common which is unsupported on Linux in .NET 6+ (PlatformNotSupportedException in Docker). Switched to SKCodec.Create (SkiaSharp - already a project dependency with Linux native assets). Only parses the image header instead of fully decoding pixels. 3. Streaming uploads instead of byte[] buffering IFormFile contents were copied into byte[] via MemoryStream.ToArray() (doubling memory during copy), then wrapped in another MemoryStream for upload. Now IFormFile.OpenReadStream() feeds directly into BlobClient.UploadAsync, with a CryptoStream wrapper computing MD5 in the same pass. Peak memory per concurrent request drops from ~200 MB to a few transfer buffers. Removed the ConvertIFormFileToByteArray helper. 4. Throttled and async-ified dashboard status pings UpdateConversionStatusDB was calling a synchronous RestClient.Execute (blocking a thread pool thread) with a freshly constructed RestClient on every successful image. Now: - Uses IHttpClientFactory (pooled connections) with PostAsync (non-blocking) - Throttled via LastReportedPercentage on the tracker - only POSTs when the integer percent advances or the run completes (~100 calls instead of 10k for a 10k-image run) - Removed RestSharp dependency from this file 5. Cached per-image organization, practice, and template lookups GetFirstOrganization, GetOrgIdForPracticeAsync, and GetTemplate were called per-image. For a 10k-image run that was 30k-50k redundant DB roundtrips for values that don't change. Added static concurrent caches: - Organization: volatile field (singleton per instance) - OrgId by practice: ConcurrentDictionary<Guid, Guid> - Template by name: ConcurrentDictionary<string, (bool, TemplateModel)> - caches both hits and misses (case-insensitive) 6. Short-circuit on status log before querying Images table CheckIfImageExists hit the Images table on every image. Now checks the already-in-memory statusLog.Status == Success first (free). The DB query is only reached for the rare crash-recovery case where status is still Pending. Medium-Impact Fixes 7. Singleton BlobContainerClient new BlobContainerClient(connectionString, container) was called per upload, re-parsing the connection string and rebuilding the HTTP pipeline each time. Now lazily cached in a static volatile field and reused across all requests. 8. Moved ConversionImageDataHelper and ImageInterface to DI Both were manually constructed via new. Registered ConversionImageDataHelper as transient in Startup.cs, injected it into ConversionService, and injected ImageInterface into the helper. Removed the now-unused CloudDbContext and AzureStorageConfig fields from ConversionService. 9. Observed metadata fire-and-forget Task.Run(() => GenerateImageMetaData(...)) swallowed all exceptions silently. Now awaited after DB image creation with a local try/catch - failures are logged in process logs but don't fail the image conversion. 10. Atomic counter mutations ProcessedCount++ and SuccessCount++ on ConversionProgressTracker were non-atomic read-modify-writes under concurrent uploads for the same run. Added backing fields with Interlocked.Increment via IncrementProcessedCount()/IncrementSuccessCount() methods. Property getters use Volatile.Read. Low-Impact Cleanup 11. Reduced process log DB writes on success ~10+ Info-level step-by-step log rows were persisted per successful image. On the success path, Info logs are now stripped and replaced with a single summary row. Error paths still save all accumulated breadcrumbs for diagnostics. 12. Simplified RetrieveStatusLog error handling Was throwing an exception on null then immediately catching it to wrap in Result. Now returns the exception directly via implicit conversion - no throw/catch overhead. What Was NOT Changed (and Why) #6 - Parallelizing sequential awaits: All DB-bound helpers share the same scoped EF Core DbContext, which is not thread-safe. Parallelizing them would require creating separate DI scopes per branch - a much larger refactor. The caching in #5 already captured the bulk of this win by making the independent lookups instant. Impact Estimates (10k-image conversion run)

What I did today: 1. Replaced RestSharp.RestClient with IHttpClientFactory (commit 6946b4e) - New SOTAResources/DatabaseInterfaces/HttpClientFactoryProvider.cs - static provider wrapping a real IHttpClientFactory with named clients (sota-cloud, sota-cloud-long, sota-logs) so CloudInterface/LogInterface get pooled HttpClients without every call-site joining DI. - CloudInterface.cs rewritten: MultipartFormDataContent for uploads, JsonContent.Create for JSON bodies, CancellationTokenSource + typed exception catches for the retry loop, plus StringContentFromValue / BuildUrl helpers. - LogInterface.cs rewritten to use HttpClient from the factory (URL + User-Agent moved into the named-client config). - SOTAResources.csproj - removed RestSharp 112.1.0, added Microsoft.Extensions.Http 8.0.1. 2. Fixed log body double-encoding (commit 3d9e430) - LogInterface.LogMessage was wrapping jsonLog in JsonConvert.SerializeObject(...) a second time, producing a JSON-encoded string where the server expected a JSON object. Server returned 500. Removed the extra serialization - now sends jsonLog directly as the body. 3. Fixed GetAccessToken serialization / timeout (commit 59409ef) - Auth request was hitting the 5-second client-level timeout on cold FusionAuth calls. Removed the per-client timeout on CloudClientName. 4. Removed client-level 5s timeout from non-upload flows (commit 542e54f) - Only the upload path needs the 5s cap, and it already enforces it via a per-request CancellationTokenSource. All other methods now use the default HttpClient timeout (~100s), matching the original new RestClient() behavior. 5. Added error-body logging to upload flow (commit a8eeb07) - Cloud upload now surfaces failure responses in the logs. 6. TestDataGenerator - use real DICOM templates from test-data/dicom-templates - Program.cs now loads all .dcm files from {AppContext.BaseDirectory}/test-data/dicom-templates and picks one at random for each file created, instead of writing a 132-byte DICOM stub. Added a needPathReplacement flag (with E:→C: defaults) to make path remapping opt-in, and moved Database.db alongside the templates under test-data/. - TestDataGenerator.csproj - copies test-data/dicom-templates/**/* and test-data/Database.db to the build output via CopyToOutputDirectory=PreserveNewest, so the tool is self-contained. Net effect - RestSharp dependency removed from the solution. - Socket pooling via IHttpClientFactory replaces the per-call new RestClient() pattern in CloudInterface. - All HTTP contract / timeout semantics now match the original RestSharp behavior end-to-end.
